origin: Goddess of magic, crossroads, and the night.
Primordial deity of shadow and darkness.
Swift-footed huntress of Arcadia.
Titan of heavenly light; father of Helios.
Personification of the moon.
Ferryman of Hades across the Styx.
Slayer of Medusa; founder of Mycenae.
Fire-breathing hybrid of lion, goat, and serpent.
Personification of non-violent death.
Final resting place for the heroic and virtuous.
Monstrous father of all monsters.
Cretan princess of the labyrinth thread.
Minor deity of the Greek pantheon known as the drinker of unmixed wine.
A figure of extreme beauty in Greek mythology representing the cycle of life and death, beloved by Aphrodite.
A composite deity of Greek mythology depicted with goat-like features, often associated with the god Pan.
A benevolent spirit in Greek mythology associated with good luck, health, and the harvest.
The personification of struggle, contest, and the spirit of competition in Greek athletic and intellectual games.
Greek personification of family feuds and the avenger of evil deeds.
The collective deities of the four winds in Greek mythology, each representing a cardinal direction.
An Arcadian deity associated with the upbringing of the god Despoina at Lycosura.
Greek god of the sun, music, archery, and prophecy, often associated with light and truth.
A group of Greek deities or spirits invoked to avert evil, bad luck, or malevolent influences.
Greek rustic god of shepherds, cheese-making, beekeeping, and olive growing.
Greek god of medicine and healing, famously depicted holding a staff entwined with a serpent.
Greek titan god of dusk, stars, and planets, and the father of the four winds.
The personification of opportunity, luck, and the favorable moment in Greek mythology.
Personification of time in Greek philosophy, often depicted as a primordial deity wielding a scythe.
The personification of gold in Greek mythology, representing wealth and precious metal.
One of the original Titans, associated with the intellect and the celestial axis of the heavens.
Greek god of festivity, revelry, and nocturnal entertainment who is often depicted holding a torch.
One of the twelve Titans of Greek mythology, associated with the constellations and the ram.
Leader of the Titans and father of the Olympian gods who was overthrown by his son Zeus.
A healing deity from the region of Paeonia often associated with solar radiance and restoration.
Greek god of wine, winemaking, fertility, ritual madness, and theatre.
A group of deities associated with the sun or solar rays in ancient Greek mythology.
The personification of mercy, pity, and compassion in Greek mythology, honored with an altar in the Athenian agora.
An ancient Greek deity of war often identified as an epithet for Ares or a separate minor war god.
A Greek epithet for Poseidon, signifying his role as the god who presides over the shore and the coast.
Greek personification of nightmares who brought terror during sleep.
An epithet for deities like Zeus or Apollo signifying one who grants prosperity or brings aid.
Titan brother of Prometheus who accepted Pandora as his wife despite warnings about the consequences.
The Greek personification of prudence, shrewdness, and careful thought.
An Orphic epithet for Phanes, representing the primordial, light-bearing creator deity who emerged from the cosmic egg.
The personification of old age and the decrepitude that inevitably follows a long life in Greek mythology.
Hellenistic adaptation of the Egyptian child deity Horus, represented as a god of silence and secrets.
Minor deity in Greek mythology, often cited as the father of the Dactyls and husband of Anchiale.
Personification of the sun in Greek mythology who drove his golden chariot across the sky each day.
Greek god of fire, metalworking, and craftsmanship who forged weapons for the Olympians.
Divine hero of Greek mythology known for his immense strength and his completion of twelve legendary labors.
Greek personification of the Evening Star and the planet Venus at dusk.
Personification of the din, clamor, and confusion of battle in ancient Greek mythology.
Legendary ancient Greek poet attributed with the composition of the Iliad and the Odyssey.
The personification of oaths and the punisher of those who break them in Greek mythology.
An epithet of Apollo meaning lord of the woods, associated with forests and woodland groves.
Greek personification of sleep who dwells in the underworld and brings rest to mortals and gods alike.
A deity associated with the Eleusinian Mysteries and the ritual procession of the torch-bearing initiates.
Titan of mortality and father of Prometheus, Atlas, and Epimetheus in Greek mythology.
A primordial river god and the first king of Argos in Greek mythology.
The Greek personification of stupidity and foolishness mentioned in the works of Aristophanes.
The personification of the din and confusion of battle, often depicted as a demon of war who thrives amidst the slaughter.
Titan of the air and the unseen, considered the father of Aura.
Thracian king who was struck with madness by Dionysus for opposing his cult.
An epithet for Zeus associated with propitiatory rites and the underworld, often depicted as a serpent.
A primordial sea deity of the Greek pantheon who was transformed into a god after falling into the ocean.
The Greek personification of satire, mockery, and unfair criticism who was expelled from Olympus for his constant fault-finding.
The personification of impending doom and the force that drives mortals toward their inevitable fate.
Greek god of dreams who shapes the forms of those who appear in the sleeper's visions.
A minor deity invoked to drive away swarms of flies during sacrifices.
Greek personification of destruction and ruin.
The personifications of dreams in Greek mythology, often depicted as winged spirits who emerge from the gates of sleep.
An obscure epithet of the Greek god Priapus, associated with the phallus and fertility rites.
Primordial deities in Greek mythology representing the physical mountains born from Gaia.
Ancient Greek deity who served as the physician to the gods and the personification of healing songs.
A pair of chthonic twin deities associated with geysers and thermal springs in Sicily.
The personification of grief and lamentation in Greek mythology, often depicted as a companion to the goddess Eris.
An ancient Greek name for the planet Saturn, personified as a celestial deity associated with the brightness of the heavens.
Primordial deity of procreation and life in the Orphic tradition, often depicted as a golden-winged being emerging from a cosmic egg.
One of the Oneiroi, the god of inanimate objects and surreal manifestations in dreams.
Greek minor deity associated with the invention of the wagon and the plow.
One of the Oneiroi, the god of nightmares who appears in the form of beasts and monsters in dreams.
The ancient Greek term for wandering stars, referring to the seven celestial bodies that moved independently against the backdrop of the fixed stars.
Greek god of wealth, often depicted as the son of Demeter and Iasion.
The personification of war and battle in Greek mythology, often associated with the violent aspects of conflict.
Greek personification of hard labor and toil, born of Eris.
Greek god of fertility, protector of livestock, fruit plants, and gardens.
Titan of foresight who defied the gods by stealing fire to bestow upon humanity.
An ancient Greek name for the planet Mars, derived from the word for fiery or burning.
A figure associated with the martial Pyrrhic dance, often linked to the mythic training of the Curetes.
A syncretic deity introduced in Ptolemaic Egypt combining aspects of Osiris and Apis with Greek iconography.
An elderly companion and tutor to Dionysus, often depicted as a wise but hedonistic satyr.
A giant who was transformed by Gaia into a fig tree to escape the wrath of Zeus.
An epithet meaning man-divine or god-man, often associated with the intersection of human and divine nature in Hellenistic theology.
The personification of boldness, insolence, and rashness in Greek mythology.
Attic deities representing the ancestors, often associated with the winds and the cycle of life.
Chthonic deity of architecture and prophecy whose subterranean oracle in Lebadeia was known for inducing terror in visitors.
A minor deity or spirit associated with good fortune and chance in Greek mythology.
An archaic chthonic deity often associated with the Orphic mysteries and the cycle of rebirth.
The personification of dedication, emulation, eager rivalry, envy, and jealousy in Greek mythology.
The king of the Olympian gods and ruler of the sky, known for wielding thunderbolts.
Greek personification of misery and sadness, often depicted as the death-mist covering the eyes of those dying.
Greek personification of gluttony and insatiable hunger.
The personification of injustice and wrongdoing in Greek mythology, often depicted as a companion to Eris.
Greek goddess of inevitable retribution and the inescapable nature of divine justice.
The personification of sloth, idleness, and indolence in Greek mythology, often depicted as a daughter of Erebus and Nyx.
The Greek personification of shame, modesty, and reverence who fled the earth at the end of the Golden Age.
Personification of the war cry in Greek mythology, often associated with the battle shout of Ares.
The personification of truth and sincerity in Greek mythology, often depicted as a woman emerging from a well.
The personification of helplessness, lack of means, and the inability to find a path forward.
An epithet of the goddess Demeter referring to her role as a protector of the Amphictyonic League.
The personification of ruthlessness, shamelessness, and lack of pity in Greek mythology.
Greek personification of inevitability, compulsion, and necessity who predates the Olympian gods.
Greek messenger goddess and daughter of Hermes who serves as the personification of messages, tidings, and proclamations.
Personification of deceit, guile, and fraud who emerged from the primordial darkness of Erebus.
A Greek goddess worshipped primarily on the island of Aegina, often associated with hunting and fertility.
Greek personification of simplicity, innocence, and ease of mind.
The personified spirits of curses in Greek mythology who pursued those who swore false oaths.
The messenger of the Titans who possessed iridescent wings and was cast into Tartarus by Zeus.
Greek titan goddess of the stars and daughter of Coeus and Phoebe.
Thracian goddess of the moon and the hunt, later assimilated into the Athenian pantheon.
A daughter of Poseidon and Amphitrite who ruled over the deep waves of the sea.
An epithet for various underworld deities, meaning the terrifying or the raging one.
An epithet of Artemis associated with the walnut tree and the village of Caryae in Laconia.
Ancient Greek primordial sea goddess representing the dangers of the ocean and sea monsters.
The three goddesses of charm, beauty, nature, and human creativity who attended to Aphrodite.
Enchantress from Greek mythology who transformed her enemies into animals on the island of Aiaia.
Anatolian mother goddess associated with wild nature, mountains, and fertility, often depicted accompanied by lions.
Greek goddess of the harvest, agriculture, and the fertility of the earth.
An ancient Greek mystery goddess of Arcadia who was the daughter of Demeter and Poseidon.
Greek personification of the sunset and the western horizon.
Greek personification of impiety, irreverence, and the neglect of religious duty.
The Greek goddess of childbirth and midwifery, daughter of Zeus and Hera.
An olive branch wrapped in wool, hung with fruits and cakes, carried in ancient Greek harvest festivals to ensure prosperity.
The personification of the Olympic truce, representing the cessation of hostilities and the sanctity of the games.
Minor Greek personification associated with the arts and poetic inspiration.
The personification of liberty and freedom in Greek mythology.
An epithet of Hecate meaning of the way, referring to her role as a guardian of paths and gateways.
Greek goddess of war who accompanies Ares into battle and delights in the destruction of cities.
Greek goddess of the dawn who rises from the edge of the world to bring light to mortals and gods.
Chthonic deities of vengeance who pursued those who had committed crimes against the natural order.
Greek goddess of the morning dew, daughter of Zeus and Selene.
Greek personification of glory and good repute often associated with the goddess Artemis.
Greek deity of the flour mill and guardian of the sacred grove at Tanagra.
The personification of piety, holiness, and reverence in Greek mythology.
The personification of Earth in Greek mythology and the ancestral mother of all life.
A group of minor deities associated with Aphrodite, often invoked in relation to procreation and the act of birth.
Three sisters in Greek mythology who shared a single eye and tooth among them.
A minor deity or personification associated with the training and physical discipline of the gymnasium in ancient Greek culture.
Greek goddess of harmony and concord, daughter of Ares and Aphrodite.
Greek personification of fate, destiny, and the unavoidable lot assigned to every individual.
Greek personification of the day and daughter of Erebus and Nyx.
Queen of the Greek gods and goddess of marriage, women, and childbirth.
An epithet of Athena meaning horse-related, reflecting her role as the inventor of the bit and the patron of horsemanship.
The goddesses of the seasons and the natural portions of time, acting as guardians of the gates of Olympus.
Greek personification of impulse, effort, and the urge to act.
Greek goddess of health, cleanliness, and sanitation, often depicted holding a serpent and a bowl.
The Hysminai were the female personifications of combat and fighting, serving as attendants to Ares on the battlefield.
An Oceanid nymph and goddess of knowledge who became the queen of Colchis.
Greek personification of vice and moral badness, often depicted as a beautiful woman who tempts individuals toward an easy but corrupt life.
An epithet for various Greek goddesses representing their role as nurturers and protectors of children.
A daughter of Helios and Neaera who served as a guardian of her father's sacred cattle on the island of Thrinacia.
An ancient nymph or divinity associated with the city of Lampsacus on the Hellespont.
Titaness of motherhood and mother of the twins Apollo and Artemis by Zeus.
The personified spirits of prayer and repentance in Greek mythology who follow behind Ate to heal the damage caused by sin.
The personified spirits of battle, carnage, and slaughter in Greek mythology who accompanied Ares to war.
Personifications of madness, insanity, and frenzy in Greek mythology.
Greek personification of drunkenness and an attendant of the god Dionysus.
The Titan personification of memory and the mother of the nine Muses.
The nine daughters of Zeus and Mnemosyne who presided over the arts and sciences in Greek mythology.
Greek goddess who enacted retribution against those who succumbed to hubris.
Ancient Greek deities representing the individual islands born from Gaia.
Nymphs of the mountain Nysa who were said to have nursed the infant Dionysus.
Primordial deity of the night and mother of many personified spirits in Greek mythology.
The Greek personification of misery, anxiety, and grief, born as a daughter of Nyx.
The personification of the retreat or rout in battle, representing the tactical withdrawal of soldiers during combat.
Greek goddess of the full moon and daughter of Selene and Zeus.
The first mortal woman in Greek mythology, fashioned by Hephaestus and endowed with a jar that unleashed suffering upon humanity.
Greek personification of consolation and solace.
An epithet of Persephone meaning queen of all, often used in mystery cults to denote her sovereignty over the underworld.
Daughter of the sun god Helios and mother of the Minotaur in Cretan mythology.
Greek personification of obedience to authority and civil discipline.
Greek personification of poverty and need, often depicted as a beggar.
Ancient Greek personification of fate or destiny, often associated with the inescapable nature of one's end.
Queen of the Underworld and goddess of springtime vegetation, daughter of Demeter and Zeus.
One of the Heliades and a daughter of the sun god Helios, her name signifies the radiant one.
Greek personification of fame, renown, and rumor, often depicted with wings and a trumpet.
Greek personification of trust, faith, and good faith in contracts and social bonds.
The personification of punishment and retribution in Greek mythology, often associated with the vengeance of the gods.
An ancient Mycenaean title meaning mistress or lady, used to address various goddesses before the establishment of the Olympian pantheon.
An archaic title meaning Mistress of Animals, referring to a goddess who commands the wilderness and its creatures.
Greek goddess of justice, specifically the enforcer of oaths and the executor of judicial sentences.
The personification of the onset or the rush of battle in Greek mythology.
Greek personification of excuses and pretexts, often invoked in the context of moral justification.
Greek personification of beggary and the state of being a destitute wanderer.
Derived from the Greek term for the weaving of epic poetry and song, associated with the tradition of the rhapsode.
Daughter of Cadmus and Harmonia who was consumed by fire after witnessing the true form of Zeus.
The personification of moderation, self-control, and temperance in Greek mythology.
Greek personification of initiation and the rites of the mystery cults.
Titaness of sight and the shining light of the sky, said to endow gold and silver with their brilliance.
Greek Titaness of divine law, order, and custom who presided over the oracles at Delphi.
The primordial elder gods of Greek mythology who ruled during the Golden Age before being overthrown by the Olympians.
Titan of rash action and violent anger who was cast into Tartarus by Zeus.
The primordial titan who personified the vast world-encircling river that the ancient Greeks believed surrounded the Earth.
An androgynous deity of Phrygian origin associated with fertility and primal chaos.
An ancient Greek term referring to the inhabitants of the island of Aegina, often associated with legendary seafarers and the myth of Aeacus.
A figure derived from Greek linguistic roots associated with the winds and high places.
A king of Arcadia and son of Elatus who appears in various Greek genealogical traditions.
The legendary founder of the Carian city Alabanda who was worshipped as a hero.
Daughter of Phylacus and mother of Jason in Greek mythology, known for her role in the lineage of the Argonauts.
A skilled craftsman of the Argonauts who fashioned the ship's mast and served as a capable commander.
An Argonaut and king of Samos who was famously killed by a wild boar after boasting he would never taste the wine from his own vineyard.
A prince of Crete and son of King Minos whose death in Athens triggered the tribute of youths to the Minotaur.
A legendary Amazonian queen known for her martial prowess and leadership in battle.
Derived from the Greek word for dill, this name appears in mythic contexts as a minor figure associated with the pastoral landscapes of ancient Greece.
A figure from Greek mythology mentioned as a king of Methymna on the island of Lesbos.
An archaic name associated with the historical figure Arminius who led a coalition of Germanic tribes against the Roman Empire.
A beggar from Ithaca who served as the town messenger and was known by the name Iros.
A son of Acheron and Orphne who served as the orchard keeper of Hades and witnessed Persephone eating the pomegranate seeds.
A legendary race of people in Greek mythology described by Megasthenes as lacking mouths and surviving solely on the scent of roots and flowers.
A Spartan hero and son of Irbus who allegedly discovered the wooden statue of Artemis Orthia.
A legendary figure in Greek tradition whose name implies a connection to the lineage or ancestors of the city.
A legendary figure in Greek mythology often associated with the Illyrian tribes and the son of Illyrius.
A legendary master thief and trickster in Greek mythology, known as the son of Hermes and grandfather of Odysseus.
A secret society of Athenian men who held nocturnal, effeminate rituals in honor of the Thracian goddess Cotys.
A figure in Greek mythology known as the daughter of Amphidamas of Opus.
A legendary youth of the Mariandyni people whose mysterious disappearance during the harvest became the subject of a traditional lamenting song.
A figure in Greek mythology known as the father of Periphetes, associated with the lineage of the early kings.
Attic hero credited with introducing the plow and the first yoking of oxen to agriculture.
A youth beloved by Apollo who was granted the gift of prophecy and became the founder of the Branchidae priesthood at Didyma.
The autochthonous first king of Athens who was depicted as half-man and half-serpent.
A legendary shepherd from Mount Othrys who was transformed into a long-horned beetle by the nymphs for his arrogance.
Son of the sun god Helios and the nymph Clymene, he was a legendary king of Rhodes.
A figure from Greek historical annals and mythic lineages, often associated with the aristocratic traditions of the archaic period.
Trojan warrior mentioned in the Iliad who was killed by Odysseus during the conflict.
Derived from the Greek word chloros meaning pale green or yellowish, traditionally associated with the fresh vitality of young vegetation.
A Thracian tribe encountered by Odysseus and his men during their return from the Trojan War.
A Babylonian nobleman mentioned in Ovid's Metamorphoses who was transformed into a hawk by Apollo.
An obscure figure from Greek legend, often associated with the sea and the tumultuous nature of waves.
A figure in Greek mythology known primarily as a son of King Amphidamas of Opus.
A figure from Greek mythology associated with the early traditions of the region of Arcadia.
A priest of Dionysus in Calydon who sought to sacrifice his beloved Callirhoe and subsequently took his own life in remorse.
A legendary hero of Salamis, often identified as a serpent or a warrior who aided the Greeks in the Battle of Salamis.
A priestess of Hera whose sons were granted the gift of peaceful death after they pulled her chariot to the temple.
A centaur of extraordinary beauty who perished at the wedding of Pirithous, causing his mate Hylonome to take her own life in grief.
A Trojan warrior mentioned in the Iliad who was killed by the hero Ajax.
A daughter of Agenor in Greek mythology who was loved by the god Ares and became the mother of Evenus.
A legendary figure associated with the early traditions of Attic hero cults and ancestral lineage.
A narrative sequence within the Iliad detailing the aristeia and martial exploits of the hero Diomedes on the battlefield.
An ancient region of Thessaly named after the daughter of Elatus, often associated with the foundation of local Pelasgian settlements.
A figure from Greek mythology mentioned as the father of the nymph Calypso.
Legendary king of Cyprus and son of Paphos who founded the city of Emathia.
A youth from Lesbos who was carried into the sea by dolphins and eventually returned as a prophet.
A son of Hippocoon who was killed by Heracles during the hero's campaign against Sparta.
A figure from Greek mythology associated with the Illyrian Encheleae tribe, often linked to the transformation of Cadmus and Harmonia into snakes.
A son of Heracles and a woman of the Dryopes, mentioned in Greek mythology as a figure associated with the lineage of the Heracleidae.
A Myrmidon commander from Dulichium who fought for the Achaeans at Troy before being killed by a falling stone thrown by Hector.
A king of Sicyon in Greek mythology who was the son of Poseidon and Canace.
An obscure figure from Greek mythology occasionally referenced in heroic genealogies.
An Arcadian warrior in the Iliad who wore the armor of King Areithous and was slain by Nestor.
A Trojan warrior mentioned in the Iliad who was killed by the hero Idomeneus.
A figure from Greek mythology appearing as a daughter of Agamemnon in certain accounts of the Trojan War cycle.
The son of Ajax the Great, named for his father's iconic broad shield.
An Oceanid nymph in Greek mythology often listed among the attendants of Cyrene.
A legendary figure in Greek tradition often associated with the early kingship of the Mytileneans.
A Lapith warrior who fought against the centaurs at the wedding of Pirithous.
A figure in Greek mythology often associated with the lineage of the Gauls or Galatians.
A legendary tribe of men who lived in the Caucasus and visited the Amazons annually to ensure the continuation of both tribes.
A legendary figure associated with the heroic lineage of ancient Greek tradition.
Daughter of Perseus and Andromeda whose name translates to slayer of the Gorgon.
A figure from Greek legend noted for his association with the sanctuary of Delphi.
A captive woman of Tenedos known for her skill in preparing restorative potions for Nestor during the Trojan War.
Derived from the Greek Hecatoncheires, referring to the hundred-handed giants of immense strength who fought for Zeus.
A derivative term associated with the forge and craftsmanship of the Olympian god of fire.
An obscure figure from Greek mythology often associated with the horse-taming traditions of the heroic age.
Spartan king and son of Oebalus who was deposed and eventually killed by Heracles for his role in the murder of Oeonus.
A figure in Greek mythology known as a traveler or wayfarer, often referenced in archaic epics.
A legendary figure from Boeotian mythology who fled Argos after accidentally killing Molurus and founded the city of Hyettus.
A centaur from Greek mythology who was known for his participation in the Calydonian Boar hunt.
A centaur who committed suicide upon the death of her mate Cyllarus during the battle with the Lapiths.
An Argonaut from Pherae who participated in the epic quest for the Golden Fleece.
A figure in Greek mythology whose name translates to high-horned, often associated with the strength and ferocity of ancient warriors.
An Athenian hero who learned the art of winemaking from Dionysus and met a tragic end at the hands of suspicious shepherds.
A legendary figure of Euboean tradition, sometimes identified as a son of Heracles or an early king associated with the founding of local cults.
A daughter of Megapenthes in Greek mythology who was known as a princess of Tiryns.
A hero mentioned in Homeric epics as one of the suitors of Penelope who fought alongside the Greeks during the Trojan War.
Greek personification of fruit and the harvest, often depicted as a youth.
In Greek mythology, Krotos was a son of Pan and Eupheme, credited with the invention of applause and the hunt.
A figure from Greek tradition often associated with the Phrygian cap and the ritualistic origins of the Korybantes.
A legendary figure associated with the foundation of the city of Lamia in Thessaly.
Daughter of Amphitryon and sister of Heracles in Greek mythology.
A figure from Greek myth who, according to legend, died of grief after her lover Orestes left her.
An obscure figure from Greek tradition often associated with the patronage of the vine and the harvest.
A name found in Greek mythology often associated with the daughters of Minyas or the Oceanids.
A figure from Greek mythology associated with the sanctuary of Artemis Leucophryene in Magnesia.
A figure from Greek mythology mentioned in the context of the Trojan War as a son of Priam.
A legendary figure associated with the Mariandyni people of Bithynia and the mournful dirge tradition in Greek myth.
An ancient Greek name associated with a warrior mentioned in the context of the battle against the Centaurs.
A princess who chose the mortal Idas over the god Apollo, preferring a human life and inevitable death.
A figure in Greek mythology known as the daughter of Euphemus and mother of Euphemus of Taenarum.
A figure from Greek mythology known as the daughter of Creon and wife of Heracles who inadvertently caused his death.
Son of Menelaus whose name translates to great grief and who was born of a slave woman.
A son of Onchestus or Apollo who died defending the gates of Thebes during the war of the Seven against Thebes.
A name derived from Greek roots meaning dark flower, often associated with figures of tragic beauty in Hellenic folklore.
A figure in Greek mythology known as a son of Lycaon and a minor hero within the context of Arcadian genealogy.
The Molionides were twin sons of Poseidon and Molione, known for their conjoined bodies and martial prowess in the heroic age.
A character in Greek mythology who was the son of Arion and a participant in the events surrounding the death of Periphetes.
A legendary Greek seer and prophet who participated in the voyage of the Argonauts and the Calydonian Boar hunt.
A Thracian king and hero who fought alongside the Amazons against the forces of Priam.
An eponymous ancestor of the Myrmidons, the legendary warriors who were said to have been transformed from ants into men by Zeus.
A legendary figure associated with the Mysian people of northwestern Asia Minor who fought alongside Telephus in the Trojan War.
A figure from Greek mythology often associated with the lineage of the early kings of Arcadia.
A figure from Greek legend noted in the context of nocturnal associations and shadowy tactical maneuvers.
One of the three Harpies in Greek mythology, whose name translates to the swift-flying one.
A figure from Greek mythology occasionally associated with the lineage of heroes and the complex genealogies of the heroic age.
A nephew of Heracles who fought alongside the hero in his campaigns against the Eurytids.
A figure from Greek heroic tradition often associated with obscure local cults and regional lineage myths.
A Trojan warrior mentioned in the Iliad who was slain by Teucer during the defense of the city.
A king of Cyllene in Arcadia who was known for his martial prowess and involvement in mythological lineage.
A figure from Greek mythology mentioned in the Catalog of Women as a king of the Epeians.
An ancient citadel in Mysia, often associated with the Greek hero who allegedly founded the city.
A minor hero of Greek legend who served as a companion to the Argonauts during their voyage to Colchis.
An eponymous hero of the Perrhaebi, an ancient tribe dwelling in the mountainous region of Thessaly.
A boatman of Mytilene who was granted youth and beauty by the goddess Aphrodite.
A herald or messenger figure in Greek tradition whose name translates to one who brings peace or a truce.
A name associated with the Phlegraean Fields, the legendary site of the battle between the Olympian gods and the Giants.
A figure from Greek mythology occasionally referenced as a son of Neleus and Chloris.
A legendary figure associated with the region of Phthia in Thessaly, the ancestral home of Achilles.
A fountain nymph of Argos associated with the purification of the Danaides.
A Pelasgian king who fell into a cauldron of boiling wine after attempting to force himself upon the priestess of Demeter.
A Macedonian king who named his nine daughters after the Muses, leading to a contest of skill between the Pierides and the Muses.
A figure in Greek tragedy and mythology, often associated with the cursed house of Atreus and the cycles of familial violence.
A loyal companion of Odysseus who was known for his gentle and trusting nature.
A figure from Greek mythology noted as the daughter of Perieres and mother of the king of Messenia.
Derived from the Greek term for a metal bubble or dross, appearing in ancient alchemical and metallurgical texts.
A figure in Greek mythology known as the father of Agastrophus and a descendant of the royal line of Messenia.
A minor figure in Greek mythology known as the father of the nymph Leucothea.
A Trojan ally and warrior mentioned in the Iliad who was killed in the defense of the city.
A Thracian king who attempted to trap the Muses in his tower and perished falling from its heights.
A centaur who fought against the Lapiths during the wedding of Pirithous.
A figure from Greek legend occasionally associated with the lineage of regional heroic figures.
An ancient name associated with the Lycian region and the mountain range of Sidymus.
An eponymous hero of the Sicani people who led his followers from Iberia to settle in Sicily.
A collection of Greek kings and princes who pledged an oath to defend the marriage of Helen and Menelaus.
Persian satrap who led the Maeonian and Lydian contingents during the invasion of Greece under Xerxes I.
A figure from Greek lore occasionally associated with the lineage of regional heroic traditions in antiquity.
A legendary tribe of pirates from the islands of Taphos who were descendants of Teleboas.
A figure from Greek mythology often associated with the lineage of kings or the ritualistic completion of sacred rites.
A figure from Greek mythology occasionally referenced in genealogies as a minor hero or descendant of noble houses.
A king of Sicyon in Greek mythology who was the son of Apis and a descendant of the autochthonous kings.
A name of Greek origin meaning godly or god-fearing which appears in various Hellenistic contexts as a virtuous figure.
A prophetic daughter of Proteus in Greek mythology known for her gift of foresight and adherence to truth.
Athenian general who led the resistance against the Thirty Tyrants to restore democracy.
A son of Orestes who reigned as king of Sparta and Argos before falling in battle against the Heracleidae.
A figure from Greek mythology mentioned as a son of the hero Heracles and Lysippe.
A figure from Greek legend associated with the geography of the Troad and the city of Tragasai.
A legendary figure associated with the founding of the city of Tlos in Lycia.
Legendary commanders of the city of Troy during the ten-year siege by the Achaeans.
A legendary king of Dodona in Epirus known from early Greek genealogical traditions.
A name derived from the Greek word for fair-haired or golden, often associated with figures of nobility in classical literature.
A legendary figure associated with the martial traditions and early warrior cults of ancient Greece.
Legendary creatures of ancient lore representing eternal or immortal entities often associated with the boundaries of the afterlife.
A Greek bogeywoman or flour-goddess used by nurses to frighten children into obedience.
A term from ancient Greek literature describing beings or tribes that practice cannibalism.
A colossal sea monster of medieval bestiaries described as a whale or island that lures sailors to their doom.
A lethal serpent of Greek legend capable of killing with a single glance or its venomous breath.
A legendary beast described by Pliny the Elder as having a bull-like head and inward-curving horns that expelled caustic fire-like dung to repel pursuers.
A monstrous boar sent by Artemis to ravage the region of Calydon until it was slain by a band of heroes led by Meleager.
A chthonic monster with a serpent tail and scorpion sting tasked by Cronus to guard the Hecatoncheires in Tartarus.
Seven herds of sacred cattle tended by the daughters of the sun god Helios on the island of Thrinacia.
A creature from Greek mythology possessing the upper body of a human and the lower body and legs of a horse.
A legendary desert serpent described in ancient texts as having horns and the ability to mimic the voices of humans to lure prey.
The multi-headed hound that guards the gates of the Greek underworld to prevent the dead from leaving.
A pair of mischievous, shape-shifting forest brothers who were turned into monkeys by Zeus for their deceitful behavior.
A golden-horned deer sacred to Artemis that Heracles pursued for an entire year to complete his third labor.
A chthonic deity in Greek mythology who resides in the underworld and is identified as the father of Menoetes.
A legendary creature from Greek accounts of Arabia said to collect cinnamon sticks to build its nest in inaccessible cliffs.
A mythical dog-wolf hybrid from Indian and Ethiopian folklore said to mimic the human voice to lure prey into the forest.
A monstrous pig that terrorized the region of Crommyon before being slain by the hero Theseus.
A legendary fish described by ancient writers as having the power to halt ships by attaching itself to the hull.
A figure from Greek folklore often associated with the restless spirits of those who died before their wedding day.
Ancient Greek female demon said to kidnap or kill children.
A legendary creature with the body of a lion and the head and wings of an eagle, often depicted as a guardian of divine treasure.
Giants of Greek mythology possessing one hundred hands and fifty heads, born to Uranus and Gaia.
A supernatural canine guardian of the underworld in Greek mythology, most notably represented by the three-headed Cerberus.
A creature with the body of a lion and the head of a hawk, often associated with divine protection in ancient Egyptian and Greek-influenced iconography.
A chimerical creature from Greek mythology depicted as the front half of a horse and the hind half of a rooster.
A pair of sea-gods with the upper bodies of human men, the lower foreparts of horses, and the tails of fish.
A legendary white, nocturnal worm found in the Indus River that was said to be indestructible and highly valuable for its silk-like secretions.
A winged serpent from Greek and Roman mythology capable of launching itself like a javelin at its prey.
Malevolent goblin-like creatures of Greek folklore that emerge from the underworld during the twelve days of Christmas.
A mischievous and shape-shifting spirit in Greek folklore known for trickery and thievery.
A tribe of man-eating giants encountered by Odysseus during his journey home to Ithaca.
A Libyan queen turned into a child-eating monster after her children were destroyed by Hera.
A small, lethal creature mentioned in ancient bestiaries whose body was said to be so toxic that it could kill a lion upon contact.
A legendary race of long-headed people described by ancient Greek geographers as living near the Euxine Sea.
Female followers of Dionysus who engaged in ecstatic frenzied rituals in the wilderness.
A supernatural being depicted in Bronze Age Cretan art, often shown as a lion-headed figure carrying a libation jug.
A night-fearing spirit from Greek folklore described as a biting monster that frightened children into obedience.
Gold-digging giant ants from Greek mythology that inhabited the mountains of India.
A legendary monster of Samos described by ancient writers as being so large and loud that its roar could cause earthquakes.
A legendary giant beast described in medieval bestiaries as a tooth-tyrant with a horn and a human-like face.
A creature from Greek mythology with the upper body of a man and the lower body of a donkey.
A creature from Greek mythology with the body of a bull and the tail of a serpent, whose entrails were said to grant the power to defeat the gods.
One of the Harpies in Greek mythology, whose name translates to swift-footed.
A legendary insect-like creature from Cypriot folklore said to live within fire and perish if removed from the flames.
A woodland deity associated with Dionysus, depicted as a man with horse or goat features.
Female counterpart to the satyr in Greek mythology, often associated with the wild and the retinue of Dionysus.
A hybrid creature with the upper body of a goat and the lower body of a fish, identified with the constellation Capricorn.
A serpentine sea monster appearing in various Greek myths, most notably as the creatures sent by Poseidon to punish Laocoön and his sons.
Ancient Greek iconography often depicted serpents as chthonic manifestations of the earth, wisdom, or guardians of sacred spaces.
A group of nymphs associated with the Spercheios River in Thessaly.
A creature with the head of a human, the body of a lion, and the wings of a bird, known for guarding the city of Thebes and posing lethal riddles.
Man-eating birds with metallic feathers that plagued the marshes of Arcadia and were defeated by Heracles.
A spectral entity in Greek mythology that frightened horses during chariot races at the Isthmian and Olympian games.
A giant fox in Greek mythology destined never to be caught, which was later turned into stone by Zeus.
One of the four original Muses in Spartan tradition, whose name signifies the charming of the mind.
A malevolent undead creature from Greek folklore that rises from the grave to terrorize the living.
A divine creature born from the blood of Medusa that serves as a mount for heroes and gods in Greek mythology.
A composite creature combining the grace of the equine form with the celestial symbolism of wings found in various mythological traditions.
One of the Harpies in Greek mythology, whose name translates to storm swift.
A serpent with a head at each end of its body, often described in Greek mythology as having venomous fangs and glowing eyes.
A hundred-eyed giant from Greek mythology who served as a vigilant, all-seeing watchman for Hera.
A powerful beast associated with King Minos that was captured by Heracles as one of his twelve labors.
A monstrous shapeshifter in Greek mythology that served Hecate and preyed on travelers.
A monstrous boar from Mount Erymanthus that was captured by Heracles as his fourth labor.
Three monstrous sisters in Greek mythology whose gaze could turn onlookers into stone.
Ancient Greek accounts describe the Libyan desert as a region teeming with legendary monsters and monstrous serpents.
A monstrous lion that terrorized the region of Cithaeron and was eventually slain by Alcathous.
A set of four man-eating horses belonging to the Thracian king Diomedes, captured by Heracles as his eighth labor.
A bull-headed monster born of Pasiphae, imprisoned within the Labyrinth of Minos.
A monstrous beast from Nemea with impenetrable golden fur that was the first labor of Heracles.
Two-headed dog of Geryon who was eventually slain by Heracles.
An ancient people described by Greek writers as living in the far north, noted for their simplicity and rejection of wealth.
A legendary fountain in Sicily whose waters were said to reveal the truth when written on a tablet and cast into the pool.
A region in Greek mythology believed to be an entrance to the underworld, often associated with dark lakes or subterranean rivers.
A secluded waterfall and pool in Cyprus where the mythological figure Adonis is said to have bathed.
The mythical island of the sorceress Circe in the Odyssey, located in the far reaches of the eastern sea.
A mythical rock in Eleusis where Demeter was said to have sat in silent mourning during her search for Persephone.
An ancient city in the region of Amphilochia, said to have been founded by the hero Amphilochus after the Trojan War.
A jagged coastal formation in Aethiopia where Andromeda was chained as a sacrifice to a sea monster.
An ancient city on the island of Lesbos named after the daughter of Macareus, famously associated with the head of Orpheus.
An ancient Greek term for a place lacking birds, often associated with entrances to the underworld or toxic volcanic craters.
A mountain in the Peloponnese where Perseus supposedly sacrificed to Zeus Apesantius.
An ancient Attic deme located on the road between Athens and Boeotia.
A rugged mountain range in the northern Peloponnese of Greece, home to the Styx river and associated with ancient Arcadian traditions.

An ancient city-state on the island of Crete that served as a significant center for bronze working.
A mountain range in Anatolia historically known as Mount Latmus, traditionally associated with the sanctuary of Endymion.
A sanctuary in Arcadia dedicated to Demeter, where the goddess was said to have hidden from Poseidon.
An ancient site associated with the region of Arcadia in the Peloponnese.
Derived from the Greek word kainos, signifying something new, fresh, or recently brought into existence.
A treacherous promontory on the Peloponnese peninsula known in antiquity for its violent storms and unpredictable currents.
The southernmost point of the Peloponnese peninsula, historically known as the location of the entrance to the underworld.

An ancient region in the Troad of northwestern Anatolia, named after the river Cebren.
A mountain town in Arcadia where the golden-horned hind sacred to Artemis was pursued by Heracles.
An ancient Greek city located in Bithynia, situated on the Bosporus opposite the site of Byzantium.
An ancient city in Epirus identified by some as the site of the Nekromanteion or oracle of the dead.
An ancient Greek city-state located on the coast of Bithynia that served as a significant port for trade in the Propontis region.
An ancient city in Arcadia, Greece, known for a spring that allegedly caused drinkers to develop an aversion to wine.

A sacred cavern on Mount Parnassus in Greece, traditionally associated with the Corycian nymphs and the god Pan.

An ancient island mentioned in the Homeric Catalogue of Ships, often associated with the Ionian group of islands.

A group of islands mentioned in the Odyssey where the one-eyed Cyclopes were said to dwell.
A mountain on the island of Delos believed to be the birthplace of the deities Apollo and Artemis.
An ancient coastal town in the region of Laconia mentioned in historical geography.
An ancient name for a location on the Bosphorus, often associated with a heifer or a calf in Greek mythology.

A mountain range on the island of Crete, traditionally considered the birthplace of the god Zeus.
A mountain in Phrygia sacred to Cybele, the Great Mother of the gods.
An ancient Greek city home to the Eleusinian Mysteries, the most famous secret religious rites of the ancient world.
An ancient border town between Attica and Boeotia that was once a sanctuary of Dionysus.

An ancient coastal city in the Peloponnese known for its strategic harbor and proximity to the cult centers of Laconia.
A mythical island in the far west where the sun sets and the cattle of Geryon were kept.

A rugged Greek island in the Cyclades named after the son of King Minos of Crete.
A legendary paradise in Greek mythology located at the edge of the world where the souls of heroes resided after death.

An ancient Greek coastal city located in the Propontis region, historically noted for its strategic position.

An ancient city in Arcadia, Greece, situated on a hill near the confluence of the Maloetas and Ladon rivers.

An ancient Thracian coastal town mentioned by Homer, located near the Strymon river.
The mythical island of the nymph Calypso where Odysseus was held captive for seven years.

A cape in the Mani Peninsula featuring a cave entrance traditionally regarded as one of the gates to the underworld.
